cards.definition: family — die Eltern (parents), die Geschwister (siblings), die Großeltern (grandparents)
wedding — heiraten (to get married), der Ehemann/die Ehefrau (husband/wife)
divorce — sich scheiden lassen (to get divorced), getrennt (separated)
friendship — ein Freund/eine Freundin (a friend), sich gut verstehen mit (to get on well with)
relationship — ausgehen mit (to go out with), sich verlieben in (to fall in love with)
housework — das Geschirr spülen (do the dishes), staubsaugen (vacuum), das Bett machen (make the bed)
breakfast — in Germany often bread, cold meats, cheese, and coffee
lunch — midday meal — can be the main meal of the day in Germany
dinner — in Germany often a lighter meal — Abendbrot (bread and cold cuts)
daily routine — aufstehen (get up), ins Bett gehen (go to bed), duschen (have a shower)

questions.question: What does 'die Eltern' mean?
questions.explanation: Die Eltern = parents. Die Großeltern = grandparents. Die Geschwister = siblings.

questions.question: What does 'heiraten' mean?
questions.explanation: Heiraten = to get married. Ich heirate nächstes Jahr = I am getting married next year.

questions.question: What does 'sich gut verstehen mit' mean?
options: C) To get on well with
questions.explanation: Sich gut verstehen mit = to get on well with. Ich verstehe mich gut mit meiner Schwester = I get on well with my sister.

questions.question: What does 'das Geschirr spülen' mean?
questions.explanation: Das Geschirr spülen = to do the dishes. Die Hausarbeit = housework.

questions.question: What is 'das Mittagessen'?
questions.explanation: Das Mittagessen = lunch (midday meal). Das Frühstück = breakfast. Das Abendessen = dinner.

questions.question: What does 'aufstehen' mean?
questions.explanation: Aufstehen = to get up. Ins Bett gehen = to go to bed.

questions.question: What is 'die Freundschaft'?
questions.explanation: Die Freundschaft = friendship. Ein Freund/eine Freundin = a friend. Mein bester Freund = my best friend.

questions.question: What does 'ausgehen mit' mean?
options: A) To go out of a building
B) To go out with (in a relationship)
questions.explanation: Ausgehen mit = to go out with (in a relationship). Ich gehe mit jemandem aus = I am going out with someone.

questions.question: What is 'das Abendessen' in Germany?
options: C) Dinner — often Abendbrot (bread and cold cuts)
questions.explanation: Das Abendessen = dinner. Traditional German Abendessen is often Abendbrot — bread with cold meats and cheese.

questions.question: What does 'duschen' mean?
questions.explanation: Duschen = to have a shower. Baden = to have a bath.

questions.question: Die Eltern means grandparents in German.
questions.explanation: Die Eltern = parents. Die Großeltern = grandparents.

questions.question: Heiraten means to get married.
questions.explanation: Ich möchte eines Tages heiraten = I would like to get married one day.

questions.question: Sich gut verstehen mit means to get on well with.
questions.explanation: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meinen Geschwistern = I get on well with my siblings.

questions.question: Das Mittagessen is dinner in Germany.
questions.explanation: Das Mittagessen = lunch. Das Abendessen = dinner.

questions.question: Das Geschirr spülen means to do the dishes.
questions.explanation: Das Geschirr spülen is a common household chore.

questions.question: Aufstehen means to go to bed.
questions.explanation: Aufstehen = to get up. Ins Bett gehen = to go to bed.

questions.question: Die Freundschaft means friendship.
questions.explanation: Die Freundschaft ist sehr wichtig für mich = Friendship is very important to me.

questions.question: Ausgehen mit means to go shopping.
questions.explanation: Ausgehen mit = to go out with (in a relationship). Einkaufen gehen = to go shopping.

questions.question: Traditional German dinner (Abendessen) is often bread with cold cuts.
questions.explanation: Abendbrot (literally 'evening bread') is a traditional lighter German dinner.

questions.question: Duschen means to have a bath.
questions.explanation: Duschen = to have a shower. Baden = to have a bath.

questions.question: Die ________ = family.
questions.explanation: Die Familie besteht aus Eltern, Geschwistern und Großeltern.

questions.question: ________ = to get married.
questions.explanation: Ich möchte heiraten = I would like to get married.

questions.question: Sich gut ________ mit = to get on well with.
questions.explanation: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meinen Eltern = I get on well with my parents.

questions.question: Das Geschirr ________ = to do the dishes.
questions.explanation: Die Hausarbeit = housework. Das Geschirr spülen is a common chore.

questions.question: Das ________ = lunch (midday meal).
questions.explanation: Das Mittagessen kann in Deutschland die Hauptmahlzeit sein.

questions.question: ________ = to get up.
questions.explanation: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf = I get up at seven o'clock.

questions.question: Die ________ = friendship.
questions.explanation: Die Freundschaft ist sehr wichtig = Friendship is very important.

questions.question: ________ mit = to go out with (in a relationship).
questions.explanation: Ich gehe mit jemandem aus = I am going out with someone.

questions.question: Das ________ = dinner.
questions.explanation: Das Abendessen in Deutschland ist oft leichter als das Mittagessen.

questions.question: ________ = to have a shower.
questions.explanation: Ich dusche jeden Morgen = I shower every morning.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

cards.definition: the present tense — ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht, wir sprechen
the perfect tense — used for completed past actions in spoken German — ich habe gegessen, ich bin gegangen
the imperfect tense — used in written German for the past — ich sprach, ich aß, es war
the future tense — werden + infinitive — ich werde sprechen (I will speak)
the conditional — würde + infinitive — ich würde gern (I would like)
the nominative case — the subject of the sentence — der/die/das/die
the accusative case — the direct object — den/die/das/die
the dative case — the indirect object — dem/der/dem/den
adjective endings change depending on case, gender, and whether a definite or indefinite article is used
word order — verb second in main clauses; verb at the end in subordinate clauses

questions.question: How do you form Futur I in German?
questions.explanation: Futur I = werden + infinitive. Ich werde morgen kommen = I will come tomorrow.

questions.question: Which auxiliary is used with movement verbs in the Perfekt?
questions.explanation: Movement and change-of-state verbs use sein in the Perfekt: Ich bin gegangen = I went. Ich bin gefahren = I drove.

questions.question: What is the accusative case used for?
options: A) The subject of the sentence
questions.explanation: The accusative (den/die/das/die) is used for the direct object — the thing directly affected by the verb.

questions.question: What does 'ich würde gern' mean?
questions.explanation: Ich würde gern = I would like. Ich würde gern nach Deutschland fahren = I would like to go to Germany.

questions.question: Where does the verb go in a German main clause?
questions.explanation: Verb second (V2) rule: in main clauses the verb is always in second position. Morgen fahre ich nach Berlin.

questions.question: Where does the verb go in a German subordinate clause?
questions.explanation: In subordinate clauses (introduced by weil, dass, obwohl, etc.), the verb goes to the END. Weil ich müde bin, gehe ich ins Bett.

questions.question: What is the nominative case used for?
options: C) The subject of the sentence
questions.explanation: The nominative is for the subject — the person or thing doing the action. Der Mann kauft das Buch.

questions.question: What is the dative case used for?
questions.explanation: The dative (dem/der/dem/den) is for the indirect object — e.g. Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch (I give the man the book).

questions.question: What is the Konjunktiv II used for in German?
options: C) Polite requests and hypothetical situations — 'would'
questions.explanation: Konjunktiv II expresses wishes, polite requests, and hypothetical situations: ich würde, ich könnte, ich hätte.

questions.question: Which case does German use after most prepositions of location (at rest)?
questions.explanation: Two-way prepositions (in, an, auf, etc.) use dative for location (at rest): Ich bin in dem Haus. Accusative for movement: Ich gehe in das Haus.

questions.question: Futur I uses werden + infinitive.
questions.explanation: Ich werde morgen lernen = I will study tomorrow.

questions.question: Movement verbs use haben in the Perfekt.
questions.explanation: Movement and change-of-state verbs use SEIN in the Perfekt: Ich bin gegangen, ich bin gefahren.

questions.question: The verb is always second in a German main clause.
questions.explanation: V2 word order: even when a time expression starts the sentence — Gestern bin ich ins Kino gegangen.

questions.question: The accusative case is used for the subject.
questions.explanation: The nominative is for the subject. The accusative is for the direct object.

questions.question: In subordinate clauses the verb goes to the end.
questions.explanation: Weil ich hungrig bin, esse ich jetzt = Because I am hungry, I am eating now.

questions.question: Konjunktiv II expresses completed past actions.
questions.explanation: Konjunktiv II expresses hypothetical situations and polite requests — 'would'. Ich würde gern = I would like.

questions.question: The dative case is used for the indirect object.
questions.explanation: Ich gebe meiner Mutter ein Geschenk = I give my mother a present. (Meiner Mutter = dative.)

questions.question: German adjective endings never change.
questions.explanation: German adjective endings change based on case, gender, and whether definite or indefinite article is used.

questions.question: Ich bin gegangen uses sein because gehen is a movement verb.
questions.explanation: Movement and change-of-state verbs take sein in the Perfekt.

questions.question: The Imperfekt is mainly used in spoken German.
questions.explanation: The Imperfekt is mainly used in WRITTEN German. Spoken German prefers the Perfekt for past events.

questions.question: Futur I = ________ + infinitive.
questions.explanation: Ich werde kommen = I will come. Sie werden fahren = They will travel.

questions.question: Movement verbs use ________ in the Perfekt.
questions.explanation: Ich bin gegangen = I went. Ich bin gefahren = I drove.

questions.question: In main clauses the verb is always in ________ position.
questions.explanation: V2 rule: subject + VERB + rest. Or time/place + VERB + subject + rest.

questions.question: In subordinate clauses (weil, dass, obwohl) the verb goes to the ________.
questions.explanation: Ich gehe ins Bett, weil ich müde bin = I go to bed because I am tired.

questions.question: The ________ case is used for the subject of the sentence.
questions.explanation: Der Mann (nominative) kauft das Buch = The man buys the book.

questions.question: The ________ case (den/die/das/die) is used for the direct object.
questions.explanation: Ich kaufe den Mantel = I buy the coat. (Den = masculine accusative.)

questions.question: The ________ case (dem/der/dem/den) is used for the indirect object.
questions.explanation: Ich gebe dem Kind das Buch = I give the child the book. (Dem Kind = dative.)

questions.question: Ich ________ gern = I would like (Konjunktiv II).
questions.explanation: Ich würde gern nach Deutschland fahren = I would like to go to Germany.

questions.question: Two-way prepositions use ________ for location (at rest).
questions.explanation: Ich bin in dem (im) Haus = I am in the house. (Dative — at rest.)

questions.question: ________ endings change depending on case, gender, and article type.
questions.explanation: Ein großer Mann / der große Mann — the article affects the adjective ending.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.
